@denik on Twitter (creator of Vimsical) made something like this and demoed it to me privately. Send him a DM and he'd probably be happy to talk about it. It was a JS framework for rendering arbitrary tree structures with arbitrary presentations based on the nesting or data of each node. A bit like React but much looser / more open.

oh man firefox’s dev tools were an absolute inspiration for me in getting codemirror 5 to work in a redux-like manner. the code is so good

<@UJUQESQ1Z> we don't have any visualizations with sizzle yet, but our theory is that all data structures are a kind of tree, tree notation is the simplest 2d text representation of trees, and so you can use Tree notation as the basic building block to then build all kinds of visualizations of trees. here's an example that takes a tree and can spit out json/xml/csv/yaml, etc

<@UJUQESQ1Z> we don't have any visualizations with sizzle yet, but our theory is that all data structures are a kind of tree, tree notation is the simplest 2d text representation of trees, and so you can use Tree notation as the basic building block to then build all kinds of visualizations of trees. here's an example that takes a tree and can spit out json/xml/csv/yaml, etc
<http://jtree.treenotation.org/sandbox/>
<http://jtree.treenotation.org/sandbox/>
In this one: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/> click any demo languages and then click "Explain", and you'll get a visualization of your tree as a 2D spreadsheet
In this one: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/> click any demo languages and then click "Explain", and you'll get a visualization of your tree as a 2D spreadsheet

ahaha i went to find the link i used but it’s your own blog post  — <https://jasonlaster.github.io/devtools/js/2017/02/21/react+codemirror.html>. from turning all my codemirror markers into react components i was then able to store the state of the markers in redux — though codemirror pre 6 makes that pretty hard — and have a global store or bookmarks that i could use to render slices of them and also to show the overall number of markers

A debugger that can jump back in time is a huge win! Not that you need FoC folks to tell you that. Even more impressive if it can step back by one line in a performant way (less than a second on a large page?) ...

I wonder how you've done it, I mean, if your JS and WebAssembly is compiled by SpiderMonkey, it's sounds like a giant feat to undo all the things that it is doing. There's a trick to it, right?

elements.text:
A debugger that can jump back in time is a huge win! Not that you need FoC folks to tell you that. Even more impressive if it can step back by one line in a performant way (less than a second on a large page?) ...

I wonder how you've done it, I mean, if your JS and WebAssembly is compiled by SpiderMonkey, it's sounds like a giant feat to undo all the things that it is doing. There's a trick to it, right?

text: Hey <@UD6EXQVM0> here's an overview -- <https://wiki.developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Mozilla/Projects/WebReplay>

attachments.title: Replay
attachments.text: Replay allows Firefox content processes to record their behavior, replay it later, and rewind to earlier states.  Replaying processes preserve all the same JS behavior, DOM structures, graphical updates, and most other behaviors that occurred while recording.

text: the best way to think about it is, is we capture just enough to be able to replay it deterministically

elements.text: the best way to think about it is, is we capture just enough to be able to replay it deterministically

text: at which point it is just a question of having enough checkpoints to be able to choose a good place to play from

elements.text: at which point it is just a question of having enough checkpoints to be able to choose a good place to play from

text: that simplifies it a lot, but that's the gist of it.

elements.text: that simplifies it a lot, but that's the gist of it.

text: and thanks!

Sorry to hear about the layoffs at Mozilla ... hoping this feature is unaffected.

"This design allows the replaying process to behave differently from the recording process, which allows for a fairly straightforward implementation of IPC and Debugger integration." Hmm, can it at least detect when divergence has occurred and inform the user? Maybe when this happens the user would like abilities like (1) detect approximately when divergence occurred, (2) replay again from the same checkpoint to find out how often the behavior changes and (3) make manual checkpoints - the user could use these to narrow down the cause of nondeterminism ... but, I'm on Windows so am more eager for that :slightly_smiling_face: Weirdly MS's time-travel debugging for Edge was demoed >4 years ago but I see no sign it was ever released (in Edge, anyhow - I see something here about Chakra + Node.js), and now they've switched to Chromium.

elements.text:

"This design allows the replaying process to behave differently from the recording process, which allows for a fairly straightforward implementation of IPC and Debugger integration." Hmm, can it at least detect when divergence has occurred and inform the user? Maybe when this happens the user would like abilities like (1) detect approximately when divergence occurred, (2) replay again from the same checkpoint to find out how often the behavior changes and (3) make manual checkpoints - the user could use these to narrow down the cause of nondeterminism ... but, I'm on Windows so am more eager for that

Weirdly MS's time-travel debugging for Edge was demoed >4 years ago but I see no sign it was ever released (in Edge, anyhow - I see something here about Chakra + Node.js), and now they've switched to Chromium.
